source: Uganda MIYCAN Guidelines 2021 (Maternal, Infant, Young Child and Adolescent Nutrition) / Uganda Clinical Guidelines 2016 Ch.19
publisher: Ministry of Health Uganda / UNICEF Uganda

entries.text: EXCLUSIVE BREASTFEEDING — FIRST 6 MONTHS (MIYCAN 2021): Every infant should be exclusively breastfed for the first 6 months of life — no water, no other foods, no formula, no juices. Breast milk alone provides everything the baby needs. KEY PRACTICES: (1) Initiate breastfeeding within the FIRST HOUR of birth. (2) First milk (colostrum) is yellowish, thick, and rich in antibodies — it is the baby's first immunization. Do NOT discard it. (3) Breastfeed on demand — whenever the baby wants, day and night. At least 8-12 times in 24 hours. (4) Correct positioning: Baby's body faces mother. Mouth wide open, taking in the whole areola (not just nipple). Lower lip turned outward. Chin touches the breast. (5) Signs of good attachment: No pain for mother. Baby has slow deep sucks with swallowing sounds. Baby is calm and satisfied after feeding. COMMON PROBLEMS: Sore/cracked nipples (improve attachment). Engorgement (feed more frequently, warm compress). Low milk (feed more to stimulate supply — milk works on supply-and-demand). Mastitis (continue breastfeeding, antibiotics if needed).

COMPLEMENTARY FEEDING 6-24 MONTHS — FATVAH APPROACH (MIYCAN 2021): From 6 months, introduce complementary foods while continuing breastfeeding up to 2 years and beyond. Follow FATVAH: F — FREQUENCY: 6-8 months: 2-3 meals + 1-2 snacks. 9-11 months: 3-4 meals + 1-2 snacks. 12-24 months: 3-4 meals + 1-2 snacks. A — AMOUNT per meal: 6-8 months: Start with 2-3 tablespoons, gradually increase to half a cup (125 ml). 9-11 months: Half a cup (125 ml). 12-24 months: Three-quarters to one cup (175-250 ml). T — THICKNESS: 6-8 months: Thick porridge, mashed foods (pureed). 9-11 months: Finely chopped, finger foods. 12+ months: Family foods, cut into small pieces. V — VARIETY: Include foods from ALL groups daily: staples (matooke, posho, sweet potato, rice), legumes (beans, groundnuts, peas), animal foods (egg, fish, liver, milk), vegetables (dodo, nakati, sukuma wiki), fruits (mango, pawpaw, orange, banana). A — ACTIVE/RESPONSIVE feeding: Sit with the child, encourage, do not force. Use separate plate. Be patient. H — HYGIENE: Wash hands before preparing food and feeding. Use clean utensils. Use safe water.

MICRONUTRIENT SUPPLEMENTATION (MIYCAN 2021 / UCG 2016 Ch.19): DURING PREGNANCY: Iron + Folic Acid (IFAS): One tablet daily (60 mg elemental iron + 400 mcg folic acid) throughout pregnancy and for 3 months postpartum. Prevents anaemia and neural tube defects. Take with food if nausea. IPTp-SP (malaria prevention): Sulfadoxime-Pyrimethamine 3 tablets from 2nd trimester. Deworming: Mebendazole 500 mg single dose in 2nd trimester. POSTNATAL MOTHER: Vitamin A 200,000 IU single dose within 8 weeks of delivery. Continue iron + folic acid for 3 months. INFANTS AND CHILDREN: Vitamin A supplementation every 6 months: 6-11 months: 100,000 IU. 12-59 months: 200,000 IU. Deworming from 12 months: Albendazole 200 mg (12-23 months) or 400 mg (24-59 months) every 6 months. Zinc for diarrhoea: <6 months: 10 mg/day for 10 days. >6 months: 20 mg/day for 10 days.

NUTRITION IN PREGNANCY (MIYCAN 2021): A pregnant woman needs an additional 300 kcal/day (about one extra meal). KEY DIETARY ADVICE: (1) Eat 4 meals per day instead of 3. (2) Eat a VARIETY of foods from all food groups daily. (3) IRON-RICH FOODS: Liver, red meat, fish, dark green leafy vegetables (dodo, nakati, buga), beans, fortified flour. Eat with Vitamin C (oranges, tomatoes) to improve absorption. Avoid tea/coffee with meals (blocks iron). (4) PROTEIN: Beans, groundnuts, eggs, meat, fish, milk, soybeans — essential for baby's growth. (5) ENERGY: Matooke, posho, sweet potatoes, cassava, millet porridge, rice. (6) CALCIUM: Milk, small dried fish (mukene/silverfish), green vegetables. (7) VITAMIN A: Yellow/orange fruits (mango, pawpaw, carrots), dark green vegetables, liver, eggs. (8) IODINE: Use iodized salt. FOODS TO AVOID: Alcohol (completely), raw/undercooked meat, excess caffeine. WEIGHT GAIN: Normal weight gain in pregnancy is 10-12 kg total.

MALNUTRITION ASSESSMENT AND CLASSIFICATION (UCG 19.2): CLASSIFICATION IN CHILDREN 6-59 MONTHS: (1) MUAC measurement: GREEN (≥13.5 cm): Normal nutritional status. YELLOW (12.5-13.4 cm): Moderate Acute Malnutrition (MAM). RED (<12.5 cm): Severe Acute Malnutrition (SAM). (2) Weight-for-Height Z-score: Normal: ≥-2 SD. MAM: -3 to <-2 SD. SAM: <-3 SD. (3) Bilateral pitting oedema: Always classified as SAM regardless of other measurements. MANAGEMENT OF SAM (UCG 19.2.2): Uncomplicated SAM (appetite present, no medical complications — LOC HC3): Outpatient therapeutic care with Ready-to-Use Therapeutic Food (RUTF/Plumpy'Nut). Amount: 200 kcal/kg/day (about 1 sachet per 3.5 kg body weight). Continue breastfeeding. Weekly follow-up. Complicated SAM (no appetite, medical complications, oedema grade 3, very low MUAC <11.5 cm — LOC HC4): Inpatient stabilisation. F-75 therapeutic milk in stabilisation phase. F-100 or RUTF in rehabilitation phase. Treat infections with broad-spectrum antibiotics. Vitamin A supplementation. Correct dehydration cautiously (different from non-malnourished). Continue until: MUAC ≥12.5 cm, weight-for-height ≥-2 SD, no oedema for 2 weeks.

LOCAL UGANDA FOOD GROUPS AND EXAMPLES (MIYCAN 2021): For a balanced diet, eat foods from ALL groups daily: (1) STAPLES/ENERGY FOODS: Matooke (cooking banana), posho (maize flour), sweet potatoes, Irish potatoes, cassava, millet, sorghum, rice, bread. (2) LEGUMES/PULSES: Beans (all types — kidney, black-eyed, soy), groundnuts (g-nuts), peas, lentils, simsim (sesame). (3) ANIMAL FOODS: Eggs, chicken, beef, goat, fish (Nile perch, tilapia, mukene/silverfish), liver, milk, yoghurt, ghee. (4) DARK GREEN LEAFY VEGETABLES: Dodo (amaranth), nakati (spider plant), buga (cowpea leaves), sukuma wiki (collard greens), spinach, cabbage. (5) ORANGE/YELLOW FRUITS AND VEGETABLES: Mangoes, pawpaw (papaya), oranges, passion fruit, pumpkin, carrots, sweet potatoes (orange-fleshed). (6) OTHER VEGETABLES: Tomatoes, onions, eggplant, green pepper, okra. (7) FATS AND OILS: Groundnut oil, simsim paste, avocado, cooking oil, ghee. TIP: A good meal has foods from at least 4 food groups. For children, add animal foods (even small amounts of egg or fish) for growth.
